============================================================
Question: For the food Scallions / Green onions: 1. What food category or botanical family does it belong to? 2. Give a brief description of the food (what it is, common forms). Cite your sources.
============================================================

1. Food Category and Botanical Family
Botanical Family: Scallions (or green onions) belong to the genus Allium1. Under the current Angiosperm Phylogeny Group classification, they are part of the subfamily Allioideae within the family Amaryllidaceae1. Historically, they were classified under the family Alliaceae or the Liliaceae (lily) family1.
Food Category: They are categorized as a vegetable and are widely utilized as an essential culinary ingredient, garnish, and seasoning23.
2. Brief Description and Common Forms
Description: Scallions are Allium plants that are strongly aromatic, releasing a distinctive, characteristic odor when cut, crushed, or chewed2. This aroma comes from complex sulfur-containing organic compounds (organosulfoxides) that are released when the plant experiences mechanical trauma24. Unlike standard onions, they are characterized by their long green stalks and lack a massive, fully developed underground bulb3.
Common Forms: Scallions are incredibly versatile and are heavily featured in everyday human dishes, soups, and sauces worldwide23. They are most commonly prepared and consumed fresh (raw), cooked, or dehydrated35.

============================================================
Question: Are there specific forms or parts of Scallions / Green onions that are toxic to cats? (e.g. skin, seeds, pits, raw form, cooked form, entire food, powder) Cite your sources.
============================================================

Scallions (also known as green onions) are toxic to cats in all forms and preparations1. Because they belong to the Allium genus, they share the exact same toxic properties as onions and garlic23.
Specific toxic forms and preparations mentioned in the sources include:
Fresh/Raw and Cooked: Cats can be poisoned by consuming fresh plant material, raw scallions, or scallions that have been cooked into dishes4.... Cooking, drying, or processing does not eliminate or reduce the toxic effects of Allium species18. Even spoiled Allium material remains toxic8.
Powdered and Dehydrated (Concentrated Forms): Dehydrated scallions, powdered cooking preparations, juices, and food preparations derived from or containing Allium species are all potentially toxic4.... Concentrated forms are often more dangerous because they pack a higher dose of toxins into a smaller amount of food.
The toxic principles (organosulfoxides) are distributed throughout the plant's cells and instantly convert into highly reactive, cell-damaging sulfur compounds when the plant experiences mechanical trauma, such as being chewed by a cat or chopped with a knife8.... Because these compounds survive all forms of processing and cooking, no part or form of a scallion is safe for feline consumption.

============================================================
Question: What are the toxic compounds or substances in Scallions / Green onions that harm cats? For each toxin provide: 1. Name of the compound 2. Chemical formula (if available) 3. Brief description of its mechanism of action in cats 4. Any notes on concentration or potency Cite your sources.
============================================================

Because scallions (green onions) belong to the Allium genus, they share the exact same toxic principles as onions and garlic12. The primary toxins are a complex mixture of sulfur-containing compounds that cause oxidative damage to red blood cells34.
Here are the specific toxic compounds found in scallions and other Allium species that harm cats:
1. N-propyl disulfide (Di-propyl-disulfide)
Name of the compound: N-propyl disulfide / Di-propyl-disulfide3....
Chemical formula: H
7
	​

C
3
	​

2

3

H
7
	​

36.
Brief description of its mechanism of action: It acts as a highly reactive oxidant that causes a marked decrease in the activity of the enzyme glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) in feline red blood cells57. This decreases the concentration of glutathione, which is necessary to prevent oxidative damage58. As a result, hydrogen peroxide oxidizes the hemoglobin, causing it to denature, precipitate, and form clumps known as Heinz bodies on the cell membrane7.... This leads to the rupture and destruction of the red blood cells (oxidative hemolysis)910.
Any notes on concentration or potency: It is described as a "very poisonous" organosulfur compound and is considered the principal toxin in Allium species5.... Cats are exceptionally vulnerable to this toxin because feline hemoglobin contains eight reactive sulfhydryl groups, making their red blood cells two to three times more susceptible to oxidative damage than those of other species5....
2. Sodium n-propylthiosulfate
Name of the compound: Sodium n-propylthiosulfate36.
Chemical formula: Not provided in the sources.
Brief description of its mechanism of action: Similar to N-propyl disulfide, it reduces glutathione concentrations in erythrocytes and significantly increases methemoglobin concentrations and Heinz body counts57. This oxidative damage ultimately leads to cell lysis and hemolytic anemia712.
Any notes on concentration or potency: This is also a "very poisonous" compound57. Notably, it has been isolated specifically from boiled Allium materials, demonstrating that these toxins survive high temperatures and cooking processes36.
3. Allyl propylisulfide
Name of the compound: Allyl propylisulfide36.
Chemical formula: H

Brief description of its mechanism of action: It is readily absorbed through the cat's gastrointestinal tract and metabolized into highly reactive oxidants that trigger oxidative hemolysis34.
Any notes on concentration or potency: It is another primary causative agent implicated in Allium-induced hemolytic anemia36.
4. Alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides (Precursors)
Name of the compound: Alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides (Organosulfoxides)413.
Chemical formula: Not provided in the sources.
Brief description of its mechanism of action: These are the non-toxic parent compounds sequestered inside the plant's cells213. When the plant experiences mechanical trauma—such as a cat chewing the scallion or a human chopping it—these precursors instantly convert into the highly reactive, cell-damaging sulfur-containing organic compounds listed above2....
Any notes on concentration or potency: These compounds are responsible for the characteristic flavor and odor of scallions413. Because they are present throughout the plant tissue and convert to toxins instantly upon damage, no part of the scallion is safe for a cat213.

============================================================
Question: What symptoms does a cat show after ingesting or being exposed to Scallions / Green onions? For each symptom provide: 1. Symptom name 2. Affected body system (gastrointestinal, renal, neurological, cardiac, dermal, respiratory, hepatic, hematological) 3. Severity: mild, moderate, severe, or fatal 4. Typical onset time (e.g. 'within 2 hours', '6–12 hours') 5. Any additional clinical notes Cite your sources.
============================================================

Because scallions (green onions) are members of the Allium genus, their toxicosis presents with the exact same clinical signs as onions and garlic1.... The symptoms result from gastrointestinal irritation and oxidative damage to red blood cells (hemolytic anemia)4....
1. Vomiting, Diarrhea, Abdominal Pain, and Drooling
Symptom name: Vomiting, Diarrhea, Abdominal pain, Drooling, and Loss of appetite (Anorexia).
Affected body system: Gastrointestinal.
Severity: Mild to moderate.
Typical onset time: Within 1 day of ingestion.
Additional clinical notes: These are usually the first clinical signs of toxicosis to appear4.... They are caused by direct irritation to the mouth and stomach, frequently resulting in dehydration4....
2. Lethargy, Weakness, and Depression
Symptom name: Lethargy, Weakness, Depression, Apathy, and Exercise intolerance.
Affected body system: Neurological (and systemic).
Severity: Moderate to severe.
Typical onset time: After a lag of several days (typically 2 to 4 days).
Additional clinical notes: These signs emerge as a secondary response to systemic hypoxia caused by the profound loss of oxygen-carrying red blood cells4....
3. Pale Mucous Membranes and Jaundice (Icterus)
Symptom name: Pale mucous membranes and Jaundice.
Affected body system: Hematological and Hepatic.
Severity: Severe.
Typical onset time: After a lag of several days.
Additional clinical notes: Pale gums indicate a lack of oxygenated blood (anemia)5.... Jaundice (yellowing of the skin or mucosa) occurs due to severe red blood cell destruction leading to hyperbilirubinemia5....
4. Tachypnea and Dyspnea (Breathing Difficulties)
Symptom name: Tachypnea (rapid respiratory rate) and Dyspnea (difficulty breathing).
Affected body system: Respiratory.
Severity: Severe to fatal.
Typical onset time: After a lag of several days.
Additional clinical notes: This is a compensatory response as the cat's body attempts to offset the decreased blood oxygen transportation capacity and resulting tissue hypoxia5....
5. Tachycardia (Rapid Heart Rate)
Symptom name: Tachycardia.
Affected body system: Cardiac.
Severity: Severe.
Typical onset time: After a lag of several days.
Additional clinical notes: Tachycardia develops as the heart pumps much faster to compensate for the impaired oxygen transportation caused by the ruptured red blood cells5....
6. Hemoglobinuria (Dark Urine)
Symptom name: Hemoglobinuria (dark, reddish, or brown urine) and Pigmenturia.
Affected body system: Renal and Hematological.
Severity: Severe.
Typical onset time: After a lag of several days.
Additional clinical notes: This is a hallmark sign of severe oxidative hemolysis5.... Damaged red blood cells rupture and release hemoglobin, which is then excreted through the urinary tract59. This can lead to secondary acute kidney injury (hemoglobinuric nephrosis)1011.
7. Cold Sensitivity
Symptom name: Cold sensitivity.
Affected body system: Neurological / Systemic.
Severity: Moderate to severe.
Typical onset time: Several days after ingestion.
Additional clinical notes: Affected cats may have difficulty regulating body temperature as a systemic effect of the progressive anemia5....
8. Allium Breath Odor
Symptom name: Onion/garlic-scented breath.
Affected body system: Gastrointestinal / Respiratory.
Severity: Mild.
Typical onset time: Shortly after ingestion.
Additional clinical notes: While not harmful itself, this symptom is specifically associated with recent ingestion and serves as a highly valuable diagnostic clue for veterinarians5....

============================================================
Question: What are the recommended veterinary treatments if a cat ingests Scallions / Green onions? List them in order of priority (most urgent first). For each treatment provide: 1. Treatment name 2. Brief description of the procedure 3. Any situation-specific notes Cite your sources.
============================================================

Because scallions and green onions belong to the Allium genus, they cause the exact same oxidative damage and toxicosis as onions and garlic. Since there is no specific antidote for Allium poisoning, treatment is focused on decontamination and supportive care1....
Here are the recommended veterinary treatments in order of priority:
1. Oxygen Therapy and Blood Transfusions (Life-Saving Stabilization)
Treatment name: Oxygen Supplementation and Blood Transfusion.
Brief description of the procedure: Providing the cat with supplemental oxygen and transfusing whole blood to restore the body's depleted oxygen-carrying capacity1....
Any situation-specific notes: This is the most urgent priority for severely affected cats presenting with life-threatening signs such as respiratory distress, systemic hypoxia, or profound hemolytic anemia (specifically when the hematocrit drops below 15–20%)1....
2. Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
Treatment name: Induction of Emesis (Vomiting).
Brief description of the procedure: Administering an emetic drug, such as dexmedetomidine (an α
2
	​

-adrenergic agonist), via intramuscular or intravenous injection to force the cat to expel the ingested plant material before the toxins are absorbed78.
Any situation-specific notes: This is highly valuable but only indicated if the ingestion was very recent (typically caught within 1 to 3 hours) and the cat is currently asymptomatic1.... Crucially, 3% hydrogen peroxide must never be used to induce vomiting in cats, as it is strictly contraindicated and can cause fatal hemorrhagic gastritis78.
3. Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
Treatment name: Activated Charcoal Administration.
Brief description of the procedure: Administering oral activated charcoal (typically 1 to 4 g/kg) to bind to and adsorb any residual toxins remaining in the stomach and intestines1....
Any situation-specific notes: This is typically administered directly after emesis to trap toxins before they can enter the bloodstream1.... However, it should be noted that its actual efficacy in binding to or preventing the production of Allium-derived sulfides is currently unknown11.
4. Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy (Systemic Supportive Care)
Treatment name: Intravenous Crystalloid Fluid Therapy.
Brief description of the procedure: Hospitalizing the cat to administer continuous IV fluids to rehydrate the animal, support blood pressure, and maintain proper renal perfusion1....
Any situation-specific notes: This treatment is indicated if the cat has suffered extensive fluid loss from severe vomiting and diarrhea, or presents with hypotension1.... Furthermore, it is an essential protective measure if the cat develops hemoglobinuria (dark urine caused by ruptured red blood cells clearing through the urinary tract), as IV diuresis helps protect the kidneys against secondary damage known as hemoglobinuric nephrosis4....
5. Gastrointestinal and Antioxidant Medications (Symptomatic Relief)
Treatment name: Anti-emetics, GI Protectants, and Antioxidant Therapy.
Brief description of the procedure: Administering anti-sickness/anti-nausea medications, stomach protectants, and sometimes antioxidant vitamins (C and E) or N-acetyl-cysteine1....
Any situation-specific notes: GI protectants and anti-sickness medications are used to manage mild-to-moderate abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea9. While antioxidants are sometimes administered in an attempt to combat the oxidative damage to red blood cells, they have shown minimal proven protective effects against Allium powder toxicosis in cats13....
